n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.role: Court reporters / stenographers (23-2011 — absent from 4-digit proxy; rebuilt as ~40% of W-2 wage bill; note large 1099 freelance population outside W-2 capture)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: ASR (Whisper-class models, Rev AI, etc.) now produces near-human-accuracy draft transcripts at <$0.30/min vs $3.50-7.00/page for human reporters. The production/transcription component (~40% of reporter labor) is substantially automatable. However, the officer-of-the-court function — administering oath, certifying the record, appearing as officer, carrying chain-of-custody liability — requires a licensed human in the vast majority of jurisdictions. 47 states permit digital recording but most still require a certified human officer at the deposition. California AB 1189 (2025-2026) is pending to allow certified digital recorders. Only Iowa, Idaho, New Hampshire historically required stenographic recording; most states allow digital. The automation fraction of 0.40 reflects: transcription draft production (fully automatable) minus the non-automatable certification/attendance function. At full AI maturity, the paid human role compresses to a thin certification wrapper.
breakdown_by_role.plausible_range: 0.25-0.55 (low: regulatory framework keeps human reporters required in most states; high: if AI achieves officer-of-the-court certification via statute, could approach 0.55)

breakdown_by_role.role: Transcriptionists / scopists / proofreaders (31-9094 Medical Transcriptionists in 4-digit proxy understates; rebuilt as ~12% of W-2 wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Cleaning ASR drafts into certified transcripts is exactly what current AI does best. UZH/Cambridge 2022 study showed hybrid AI+human model at 98.5% accuracy. Human review compresses to a QA pass for exceptions, legal formatting, and certification. 85% automatable — the 15% residual is final certification review and edge-case corrections for accents, technical terminology, and multi-speaker disambiguation.

breakdown_by_role.role: Scheduling / calendar / production coordination (proxy 43-4051 CSR at 25.9% overstates via contact-center contamination; rebuilt as ~20% of W-2 wage bill for 561492 coordination staff)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Job scheduling, reporter assignment, transcript production tracking, exhibit management, and delivery logistics are routine coordination functions. Modern court reporting platforms (Veritext's proprietary portal, Lexitas technology stack) already automate significant portions. 80% automatable — the 20% residual is exception handling, reporter relationship management, and last-minute coverage coordination.

breakdown_by_role.role: Management / sales (11-1021 General and Operations Managers + 41-3091 Sales Representatives; proxy shares used as starting point, scaled to ~15% for 561492)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Law-firm relationship management and litigation-support selling are primarily relationship/judgment work. Proposal generation, CRM updates, invoicing, and reporting automate. 30% automatable reflects the operational/administrative component.

breakdown_by_role.role: Captioning / CART realtime staff (23-2011 subset — captioners; rebuilt as ~8% of W-2 wage bill; this code also includes ADA-driven CART services)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: ASR captioning already substitutes at the low end — education, webinars, internal corporate meetings. Legal/broadcast realtime captioning and CART for ADA compliance holds longer due to accuracy requirements, liability, and real-time correction needs. ADA demand is growing (positive for this sub-segment). 45% automatable: the batch/education end is near-fully automated; the legal/broadcast realtime end persists with human oversight.

breakdown_by_role.role: Other admin / residual (IT, billing, clerical — remaining ~5% of W-2 wage bill)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Billing, accounts receivable, IT support, and general administrative functions. Near-fully automatable for routine tasks; 35% residual for exceptions and vendor management.

pi_dist.rationale: Court reporting agencies own the law firm's preferred-vendor slot and scheduling calendar — more durable than BPO contact centers because: (1) agencies assign reporters and handle all scheduling, eliminating client coordination burden; (2) relationships are long-standing, especially with mid-size law firms that use a consistent preferred agency; (3) transcript delivery integration with law firm case management systems creates switching friction. However: (a) certified reporters can take law-firm relationships directly when they leave an agency (reporter mobility limits pi_dist); (b) per-page pricing means AI-driven cost compression flows to clients or competitors if a digital agency bids lower page rates; (c) remote deposition platforms (Zoom-integrated ASR with certified digital reporter) allow some bypass; (d) large law firms consolidate vendor lists to national providers (Veritext, Lexitas) in competitive national-account pricing. pi_dist = 0.40: meaningful relationship ownership but constrained by reporter mobility and page-rate competition.
pi_dist.plausible_range: 0.30-0.55 (low: reporter mobility and digital bypass risk; high: sticky preferred-agency calendars in smaller law firm markets)
sources: Jackimwoods.com: Insights on Court Reporting Acquisitions 2023-2025 (https://www.jackimwoods.com/tag/court-reporting/)
TranscribeLegal AI: Legal Transcription Pricing Guide 2026 (https://transcribelegal.ai/legal-transcription-pricing-guide.html)
Esquire Deposition Solutions: Digital Reporter Service Rules by State 2025

pi_moat.rationale: Meaningful but shrinking moat. State CSR licensing requirements provide genuine regulatory barrier: in most states, only a licensed court reporter (or certified digital reporter) can certify a deposition transcript. HIPAA and data-security certifications add compliance moat for medical malpractice and health litigation work. Agency scheduling systems and reporter networks create network-effect moat in local markets (you need a reporter available in the right location). However: (a) 47 states now permit digital reporting as of 2025 — the moat is draining as certification expands to digital reporters (AAERT pathway); (b) remote deposition platforms reduce geography-based moat; (c) clients can bypass agencies entirely in many states by using a notarized recording with ASR transcript; (d) the stenographer shortage itself is accelerating rule changes (California AB 1189 pending). pi_moat = 0.35: genuine regulatory-certification moat stronger than contact centers, but actively eroding as statutes adapt to shortage.
pi_moat.plausible_range: 0.25-0.50 (low: rapid rule evolution and digital certification expansion; high: core CSR licensing persists in major jurisdictions)
sources: Lexology: Georgia digital reporter law 2026 effective date (https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=f6633c46-a8ab-4bc4-95c6-98a0ccca081f)
Esquire Deposition Solutions: Digital Reporter Rules by State 2025
Stenoimperium: Why Digital Recorders Are Not Court Reporters in California (https://stenoimperium.com/2025/07/21/why-digital-recorders-are-not-court-reporters-and-why-that-matters-in-california/)
NCRA / AAERT: Certification pathway documentation

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct = 0.35 (estimated 35% of agencies materially using AI in transcription production workflows — large national agencies (Veritext, Lexitas, Magna) already using AI-assisted transcription; many smaller agencies using commercial ASR tools (Sonix, Rev.ai, Verbit)); historical_analog: CAT (Computer-Aided Transcription) software adoption in court reporting: stenotype to digital CAT became standard from late 1990s to early 2000s (~10-year window from introduction to majority use). Voice recognition (Dragon NaturallySpeaking in legal context) also ~10 years to broad adoption. AI is moving faster because: (1) the stenographer shortage makes AI adoption existential for capacity — agencies cannot source enough human reporters; (2) AI transcription cost ($0.15-0.30/min) vs. human reporter cost ($3.50-7.00/page) is overwhelmingly compelling; (3) competitive pressure from digital-first entrants (Verbit, digital agencies) is visible in the market. Derivation: from 35% current to 50% target = 15pp gap. At CAT-analog pace (10 years for 30pp gap), this would be 5 years. But given stronger market forces (shortage + cost), compressing to 3 years. t50 = 3 years (2026→2029).
t50_years.plausible_range: 2-5 years (low: shortage pressure plus cost economics accelerate; high: regulatory requirements slow AI-only workflows; larger gap if AI certification rules stall)

current_adoption_pct.source: AAERT 2025 Court Reporting Industry Trends Report summary; Rev.com court reporter shortage analysis; Verbit digital reporting industry reports
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: Digital court reporting growing as reliable solution to stenographer shortage; large national agencies (Veritext, Lexitas, Magna) and digital-first platforms (Verbit, Stenograph AI tools) materially using AI in transcription; AAERT reports growing digital reporter certification; workforce declined 21% over last decade; fewer than 300 new reporters enter annually vs 1,000 retirements

historical_analogs.value: 10 years (CAT software adoption in court reporting, late 1980s introduction to ~50% majority use by late 1990s; voice recognition for transcription, similar 10-year adoption window)
historical_analogs.source: NCRA historical reporting / NCRA Court Reporting Industry Outlook (Ducker Report 2013-14); general CAT software adoption history
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Ducker Report (2013-14, NCRA-commissioned) established historical reporter workforce trends; CAT (Computer-Aided Transcription) became the industry standard by the early 2000s — approximately 10-year transition from minority to majority adoption from late-1980s introduction

owners_60plus_pct.source: Jackimwoods.com Court Reporting M&A Insights; NCRA workforce data; speechmatics court reporter shortage analysis 2024
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Most owners of court reporting firms are in their late 50s or older and are beginning to think about retirement (jackimwoods.com); 81% of stenographers are over 50 years old, average age 56 (NCRA/AAERT 2025 data); more than 70% of the workforce is age 45 or older (US Legal Support blog)

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Succession shortage is extensively documented for this industry: NCRA and AAERT report fewer than 300 new reporters entering the field annually vs. 1,000+ retiring each year; 74% plummet in stenography school enrollment; nearly half of stenography programs closed. Jackimwoods.com (court reporting M&A specialist) explicitly states most firm owners are in late 50s or older, creating a double succession problem: owner cannot find a buyer from within the profession because the talent pipeline is exhausted. The AAERT 2025 Industry Trends Report confirms the workforce crisis creates urgent succession pressure for firm owners. California alone reported 72% of hearings had no verbatim record due to reporter shortage.

active_consolidators.source: Stenoimperium Era of Aggregation article Aug 2025; jackimwoods.com Court Reporting Acquisitions 2025; PE Hub / Harris Williams deal announcements
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Veritext (Leonard Green + CVC Capital Partners) — 6 acquisitions in 2025 alone, 40+ total over tracked period; Lexitas (PE-backed) — 28 acquisitions total, active 2024-2025; Magna Legal Services (CIVC Partners PE) — 3 acquisitions in 2025; US Legal Support (PE-backed) — 6+ transactions in tracked period. At least 4 PE-backed platforms actively making add-on acquisitions in court reporting specifically in last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: Jackimwoods.com Court Reporting Acquisitions Insights 2025; court reporting valuation multiples by EBITDA range
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Court reporting agency EBITDA multiples by size: $500K-$1M at 3.0-5.0x; $1M+ at 4.0-6.0x EBITDA. For n_band firms (EBITDA $1.1M-$7.2M), median buy-side multiple estimated at 5.0x — midpoint of $1M+ range, reflecting AI uncertainty discount from higher historical multiples.

exit_mult.source: Veritext CVC Capital Partners investment (Harris Williams); PE platform exit comp analysis for legal services BPO with national scale and technology investment
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Veritext received significant investment from CVC Capital Partners (no multiple disclosed); comparable PE-backed legal services platforms with compliance certifications and technology differentiation trade at 8-12x EBITDA at exit. Applied 8.0x as conservative exit estimate given AI headwinds for the broader platform category, consistent with the lower end of comparable legal services BPO exit range.

pricing_structure.value: Per-page ($3.50-7.00/page for original transcript, $0.50-2.00/page for copies); appearance fee ($250-500/half-day, $400-800/full-day); expedited delivery surcharge (50-100%); per-minute AI transcription ($0.15-0.30/min). Official/court work subject to Judicial Conference maximum rates (caps upside). Commercial deposition market sets market pricing. AI transcription dramatically cheaper (~$0.15-0.30/min vs $3.50-7.00/page equivalent) — cost floor collapsing but agencies cannot reduce per-page rates immediately due to sticky law-firm billing relationships.
pricing_structure.source: TranscribeLegal AI Legal Transcription Pricing Guide 2026; CourtScribes Certified Court Reporter Cost Guide 2026; McCorkle Depositions pricing guide
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Court reporters charge $3.50-$7.00 per page for original transcripts; AI transcription $0.15-$0.30 per minute; AI-assisted court reporting can reduce per-page costs 40-60%; a firm transcribing 10 depositions per month could spend $5,000+ with court reporters or under $150 with AI transcription

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.14608, representing 14.61 percentage points of revenue freed. A = 3.33 (below 5). Cross-check 1 condition is 'uplift < 3pp while A >= 5' — does NOT trigger (uplift is 14.61pp, well above 3pp; A is below 5). Consistent: moderate V with moderate A reflects an industry where AI automation is real but constrained by regulatory certification requirements and a labor_share of only 0.2728 (agencies are intermediaries; 1099 freelance reporters outside the W-2 base further reduces the measured labor exposure). No revision needed.

terminal_value.justification: The paid product is a certified verbatim record of a legal proceeding. Mature ASR produces the verbatim text at near-zero marginal cost. What remains purchasable is the certification, chain-of-custody, and oath-administration by a licensed officer. 47 states permit digital recording but most require a certified officer present. Agencies transition from staffing stenographers to staffing certified digital reporters who certify AI-produced transcripts — the agency's production cost collapses (margin story) while the per-page revenue line compresses from digital competition. Some sub-segments melt (low-end captioning), others grow (ADA-driven CART). Deposition transcript demand persists through legal proceedings, insulated from direct AI bypass by court rules requiring certified records. Classified PRESSURED: the certified legal record product survives AI maturity but at compressed margins and per-unit price; not MELTING (unlike contact centers, the product requires a human in the certification chain per most state statutes).

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was computed from a rebuilt 561492-specific role structure, explicitly noted as replacing the 4-digit 5614 proxy (which is dominated by contact-center occupations absent from 561492). The 23-2011 Court Reporters and Simultaneous Captioners occupation — the production core of 561492 — does not appear in the top-10 of the 4-digit proxy at all. Automatable fractions were set based on: ASR accuracy evidence (UZH/Cambridge 2022, 98.5% with AI+human hybrid), state-by-state digital reporting rules (Esquire March 2025 state-by-state guide), and industry structure evidence on the agency intermediary model. A (t50=3 years) was derived from current_adoption_pct (35% material use estimate from AAERT/industry evidence) and CAT-software historical analog (10-year window), adjusted downward for workforce-shortage pressure. No PE deal activity or consolidation evidence was used for V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Court reporting agencies bill per-page for transcripts ($3.50-7.00/page original) and per appearance/half-day for reporter attendance ($250-500/half-day). When AI collapses production cost to <$0.30/min equivalent, the spread between AI cost and per-page billing rate initially accrues as margin to the agency or to digital-first competitors. However: (1) competitive digital agencies (Verbit, AAERT-certified digital reporter services) bid lower page rates, pressuring incumbent agencies to reduce; (2) law firms with purchasing power demand rate reductions as AI becomes visible in the market; (3) statutory rate schedules (Judicial Conference caps for official court work) prevent price increases but allow competitive discounting. Unlike contact centers (where AI eliminates billed volume entirely), court reporting agencies benefit from both cost reduction (AI transcription) and per-page revenue persistence (legal proceedings don't disappear). pi_dist=0.40 captures moderate agency control of client flow; pi_moat=0.35 captures regulatory certification barriers that protect the per-page revenue line from full digital bypass. Pricing-model deflation is real but less severe than contact centers: page rates are under pressure from below (digital competitors), not eliminated from above (the legal proceedings requiring certified records continue).

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: t50=2 gives A=5.00, S~3.87; t50=5 gives A=2.00, S~2.90. Range of S: ~2.9-3.9. Central estimate S=3.31 at t50=3.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: reporter fraction 0.25-0.55 → total ai_replaceable_share ~0.46-0.62; V ranges ~4.99-6.69
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: V=4.99 gives S~3.11; V=6.69 gives S~3.51. Meaningful but not decisive — regulatory certification requirement sets a floor on non-automatable reporter fraction.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0.45-0.65 (both yield OW=0.9 before succession bonus; only affects whether succession bonus triggers)
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Succession bonus already triggered (true) so only the clamped OW of 1.0 matters. No score impact within this range since the clamp binds at 0.9 and the bonus brings it to 1.0 regardless.
pi_dist=0.30 gives C=1.05, S~3.09; pi_dist=0.55 gives C=1.925, S~3.61. Range ~3.1-3.6.

evidence.reviewer_note: Role mix rebuilt entirely from 4-digit 5614 proxy: the dominant production occupation (23-2011 Court Reporters and Simultaneous Captioners) is absent from the proxy top-10 entirely because the proxy is dominated by contact-center codes. The rebuilt structure uses prompt-provided hypotheses for role shares (40% reporters, 12% transcriptionists, 20% scheduling/coordination, 15% management/sales, 8% captioners, 5% residual). The critical caveat: labor_share=0.2728 captures only W-2 workers. A large fraction of court reporters are 1099 freelancers paid per-page/per-appearance through agencies. The agency P&L is an intermediary spread on freelance production — automating transcription changes the freelancer's income before the agency sees margin impact. This means the true labor exposure of 561492 agencies to AI is higher than V_raw=0.1461 suggests (1099 freelance payout is effectively a cost-of-goods, not captured in W-2 labor_share). V = 5.84 likely understates true AI value capture opportunity for the agency. B is low (n_band=60 is a very small target universe) but OW=1.0 and active PE consolidation (Veritext/CVC/Leonard Green, Lexitas PE-backed, Magna/CIVC, US Legal Support) drive respectable B=6.03. S=3.31 reflects a mid-tier opportunity: genuine AI automation value, meaningful succession-driven seller motivation, active consolidation, but constrained by small target universe (n_band=60), moderate capture (C=1.40), and moderate adoption speed (A=3.33).

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $458,147 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561510); class <5: 6,236 firms, avg revenue/firm $469,518 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,480 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 689 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,063,269 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $322,902 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 253 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,174,640 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $653,331 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 202 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,264,381 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,293,376 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,659,729 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,424,248 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $558,919,417 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $87,470,889 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 250
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Transactional portion — fare/rate search, booking execution, rebooking, disruption handling — is squarely within current AI capability (~40% of task time). The persistent slice is trusted-advisor curation for complex/luxury/group trips and supplier-relationship leverage. Many leisure advisors are 1099 ICs; the automatable transactional tasks are the ones most at risk of consumer AI bypass. Split: 40% automatable, 60% advisory/relationship/judgment.

breakdown_by_role.role: Reservation and Transportation Ticket Agents and Travel Clerks (43-4181)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Fulfillment and servicing work — queue management, ticketing, schedule changes — is the most directly automatable line. Large TMCs are already deploying AI for this. Small residual for complex exception handling requiring human judgment.

Inbound call handling, status queries, standard change requests — heavily automatable by AI voice/chat agents already deployed in the sector. Complex complaint resolution and duty-of-care situations require human judgment (~25%).

Supplier negotiations, client account management, and staff oversight persist as human work. Ops reporting, performance dashboards, commission reconciliation tracking automate. 25% reflects the automatable reporting/analytics slice.
Physical, in-person guiding on tours is not AI-replaceable. A 2% token fraction for documentation/preparation tasks that might be AI-assisted.

Corporate-account selling is relationship work requiring human presence. Lead handling, proposal drafting, and follow-up automate. Marketing content generation automatable. 30% captures the automatable pipeline/content slice.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Marketing content, campaign analytics, and competitive research automate substantially. Strategic planning and novel insight generation less so. 50% is balanced given the mid-range automation potential of this role.

AI-assisted coding accelerates developer productivity but does not replace core engineering judgment for platform architecture. 25% reflects productivity augmentation. Note: this wage_share is concentrated in OTA/TMC platform tier, not small-agency band — reviewer note flags the heterogeneity.
Office coordination, scheduling, and workflow tracking partially automate; people management and exception handling persist. 35% for the automatable administrative coordination portion.

Invoicing, commission tracking/reconciliation (notoriously manual in host agencies), document handling — near-fully automatable. 80% with 20% residual for exception handling and non-routine correspondence.

The unlisted 37.87% of wage bill is the administrative back-office tail including commission reconciliation, invoicing, document management, and mixed support roles not individually named in OEWS data at this level. The prompt indicates this is predominantly admin/back-office. 55% automatable reflects a blended rate: high for pure admin, lower for any miscellaneous professional roles in the tail.

pi_dist.rationale: Mixed client ownership picture. At the $1-10M band (mid-size TMCs, host agencies, niche specialists): (1) Corporate TMC clients have moderate firm-level ownership via duty-of-care contracts, policy compliance and expense management integration; (2) Leisure clients are personal to the advisor, especially for 1099 ICs who can re-affiliate freely — the client book does not transfer cleanly in an acquisition; (3) GDS, ARC/IATA accreditation, and consortia (Virtuoso, Signature, Ensemble) hold the negotiated commission tiers that give value, but these aggregate at the host-agency level not the individual buyer. Revenue is mostly supplier-commission (not cost-plus billed), so AI labor savings accrue to the operator margin rather than being passed through as price reductions — this somewhat supports pi_dist. The 0.40 reflects meaningful but incomplete firm-level client control, reduced by advisor churn risk and bypass threat from consumer AI booking tools.
sources: Host Agency Reviews 2024 Independent Travel Advisor Research Report — 71% of ICs report travel as primary income; client-book portability discussed
GBTA AI in Corporate Travel survey, March 2026 — duty-of-care compliance as TMC retention driver
Travel Weekly industry coverage — 1099 IC re-affiliation and agency acquisition client retention

pi_moat.rationale: Barrier strength is moderate. ARC/IATA accreditation provides legitimate entry barrier and confers GDS access and supplier commission eligibility. Consortia membership (Virtuoso, Signature, Ensemble) creates differentiated commission tiers (15-20% vs standard 10-12% on cruise/hotel). Switching costs exist in corporate managed travel (duty-of-care, reporting integration, policy enforcement). However: (1) the industry has already been partially melted once by OTAs — the commodity transactional tier is gone; (2) Phocuswright reports 56% of U.S. travelers now use AI for trip planning, with 39% using GenAI for at least one trip in 2024 — the consumer bypass risk is real and growing; (3) on the corporate side, AI travel management tools (Navan, TravelPerk) are themselves displacing traditional TMCs at the SMB end. The 0.40 reflects genuine but limited moat in the advisory specialty/corporate compliance niches vs the broader transactional exposure.
sources: Phocuswright AI in travel research 2024-2026 — 39% of U.S. travelers used GenAI for at least one trip in 2024; 56% in past 12 months as of 2026
Host Agency Reviews commission rate data 2024 — cruise 10-16% base, consortia preferred 15-20%
GBTA March 2026 — corporate duty-of-care as retention driver

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ~15% of firms in the n_band materially using AI in automatable functions (conservative given GBTA finding that 58% of corporate travel buyers say AI has had 'little or no impact' as of March 2026, and Phocuswright finding that AI awareness is high at 86% among advisors but adoption remains limited). Historical analog: GDS/online booking diffusion — internet bookings exceeded GDS for the first time in 2006, from approximately 5% in 1998, a ~8-10 year curve to 50%+ adoption. AI adoption in services is tracking faster given lower deployment costs and SaaS availability, roughly 60-70% of the analog pace. Derivation: from 15% today (2026) to 50% target, with AI-era diffusion curve compressing the GDS analog from ~8 years to ~6 years. This is consistent with AI tools for scheduling, rebooking, and customer handling already being deployed at large TMCs — the lag at the small-agency level (~6 years from 50% floor) reflects the heterogeneity and slow-follower tail characteristic of small-business adoption. t50 = 6.0 years (2026 to 2032).

current_adoption_pct.source: GBTA AI in Corporate Travel Research, March 2026; Phocuswright AI adoption in travel 2024-2026
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 58% of travel buyers say AI has had little or no impact on their programs to date (GBTA March 2026); 86% AI awareness among travel advisors but adoption remains limited (Phocuswright)

historical_analogs.value: GDS/online booking technology: internet bookings exceeded GDS volumes by 2006, from ~5% in 1998 — approximately 8-10 years to 50%+ adoption in the travel intermediary channel
historical_analogs.source: AltexSoft History of Flight Booking; Phocuswire Brief History of Travel Technology
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: In 2006, the volume of internet reservations exceeded GDS reservations for the first time; internet bookings were 5% of U.S. airline tickets in 1998

owners_60plus_pct.source: Travel Weekly Travel Industry Survey 2023; Host Agency Reviews Independent Travel Advisor Research Report 2024
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 57% of travel advisors surveyed are older than 55 (Travel Weekly 2023 Industry Survey); median advisor age dropped to 55 in 2024 from 60 previously (HAR 2024 Report)

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Travel Weekly 2023 survey documents an aging advisor workforce (57% over 55) but no formal succession shortage for the agency-owner tier is documented in ASTA or Travel Weekly primary research. The industry narrative notes aging owners but lacks a specific documented succession gap for the $1-10M agency band.

active_consolidators.source: Business Travel News M&A coverage 2024-2025; Skift M&A tracker 2025
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Notable 2025 acquisitions: Direct Travel acquiring ATPI, Globespan acquiring Key Travel US, Trip.Biz acquiring Key Travel EMEA, Amex GBT completing CWT acquisition. Large-cap strategic acquisitions dominate; PE add-on activity in the $1-10M EBITDA host/mid-TMC band is limited.

buy_mult.figure_quoted: Travel agencies transact at an EBITDA multiple between 3.35x and 4.11x; median buy multiple approximately 3.97x for established agencies

exit_mult.source: Business Travel News 2025 M&A market intelligence; FE International agency valuation guide
exit_mult.figure_quoted: TMC sellers looking for 5x to 7x EBITDA (traditional) to 5x-8x EBITDA (B2B tech-enabled travel management). Mid-point of 6.5x used for $10M+ EBITDA platform exits in the managed travel segment.

pricing_structure.value: Primarily supplier-paid commissions (~70-80% of net revenue): cruise lines 10-16% of commissionable fare (preferred consortia 15-20%), hotels 8-15%, tour operators 10-20%, airlines near-zero base (replaced by per-ticket service fees $40-$60 domestic, $60+ international). Client-facing service fees for planning/consulting (~20-30% of revenue for advisors charging fees). Over 50% of advisors now charge service fees directly.
pricing_structure.source: Host Agency Reviews Travel Agent Commission Guide 2024; Host Agency Reviews Independent Advisor Research 2024
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Cruise commissions 10-16% base, 15-20% through preferred partnerships; hotel 8-15%; air non-commissionable with $40-$60 service fees. More than half of travel advisors now charge service fees; hosted advisors charging fees earned 42% higher income on average.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp=9.81pp is well above the 3pp floor; A=1.667 (slow adoption, t50=6 years) is below 5, so no cross-check tension. The pairing of meaningful AI labor value with slow small-agency adoption is internally consistent: large TMCs are already deploying AI but the n_band target (250 mid-size firms) lags by years.

terminal_value.justification: The 561510 product is under structural pressure but not in full melt. (1) AI CUTS OPERATOR COSTS: automating back-office, rebooking, and customer service tasks directly reduces labor costs and improves margin on unchanged commissions — this is real and bounded, worth ~10pp of revenue. (2) AI THREATENS THE PRODUCT: consumer AI trip planners (Phocuswright: 56% of U.S. travelers used AI for at least one trip in the past 12 months) increasingly substitute for transactional agency services. Supplier commissions on the transactional side are at risk of following the air-commission zero-out precedent — once AI booking agents can acquire customers directly, cruise lines and hotel chains have incentive to reduce or eliminate intermediary commissions. The corporate segment is more DURABLE because duty-of-care compliance, policy enforcement, negotiated rates, and expense management integration create structural demand that AI booking alone cannot satisfy. The leisure complexity-niche (luxury, group, cruise specialists) is similarly more durable. Net classification: PRESSURED — demand for the advisory/compliance function persists but the transactional volume and associated commission revenue are under multi-year structural decline. Not MELTING because the compliance and complexity niches are genuinely durable; not DURABLE because supplier commission structures face the air-precedent dynamic and consumer AI bypass is measurably accelerating.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was set from the OEWS role wage_shares and declared within-role automatable fractions based on task analysis of what AI can currently do (booking execution, queue management, invoicing, rebooking). A was set from Phocuswright and GBTA survey data on current AI adoption rates and from the GDS/online-booking historical analog. No deal activity or consolidation trends were referenced when setting V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Revenue is predominantly supplier-paid commission (cruise 10-16%, hotel 8-15%, tour operators 10-20%) plus client service fees. This is NOT a cost-plus or hourly billing model — AI efficiency gains from automating back-office and fulfillment labor accrue to the operator as margin improvement, not as automatic price reductions passed to clients. However, this favorable structure is offset by an indirect competitive mechanism: AI booking tools allow supplier bypass, potentially eroding the commission base itself. pi_dist=0.40 reflects incomplete firm-level client control (IC advisor churn, consumer bypass risk); pi_moat=0.40 reflects genuine but limited barriers (ARC/IATA accreditation, consortia access, corporate compliance) against growing direct-booking and AI-planning substitution.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=4: A=2.5 vs A=1.667 at t50=6; S rises from 2.93 to approximately 3.35. At t50=9: A=1.111; S falls to approximately 2.56. The t50 range spans roughly ±0.4 in S.
pi_dist=0.30 gives C=1.2 vs C=1.6 at 0.40; S falls from 2.93 to approximately 2.69. pi_dist=0.55 gives C=2.2; S rises to approximately 3.18. Range of approximately ±0.25 in S.
At consolidators=2: CFD=0.239, B=6.08, S approximately 2.94. At consolidators=6: CFD=0.423, B=5.65, S approximately 2.87. Impact is modest given CFD enters via a dampening formula.

evidence.reviewer_note: Severe heterogeneity confirmed: NAICS 561510 spans mega-OTAs (48 firms averaging $559M revenue dominating the $35.6B receipts) down to 6,236 micro leisure agencies under $500K revenue. The n_band=250 target (20-99 and 100-499 employee classes) is mid-size TMCs, host-agency networks, and niche leisure specialists — scored here, not Expedia-tier nor solo advisor. The OEWS 15-1252 Software Developer wage_share of 5.7% is concentrated in the OTA/TMC-platform tier and is not representative of the small-agency band; this inflates the remainder calculation slightly. The gross-vs-net revenue trap (agencies reporting gross bookings vs net commission) applies to the SUSB receipts data — the Damodaran 15.65% EBITDA margin may overstate or understate actual margins for the commission-intermediary model; acknowledged but dataset used as given. COVID distortion (2022 IPS labor_share on partial recovery): the 0.2099 labor_share may be atypically low or high vs steady-state; noted.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

breakdown_by_role.rationale: State-licensed applicators physically treating structures — the core product cannot be AI-replaced. Route optimization AI can reduce drive time (5-15% savings) but does not eliminate the visit. Smart traps and remote monitoring reduce visit frequency on some commercial accounts (demand compression, not labor substitution). The 5% automatable fraction captures service documentation, digital service record entry, and smart-trap monitoring reporting tasks that AI tools can assist.

Scheduling, dispatch coordination, billing, renewal processing, and records management — the automatable core. AI scheduling agents and CRM automation (FieldRoutes, PestPac, WorkWave AI features) already handle the majority of these tasks. Small residual for exception handling and non-routine customer interaction.

Multi-branch reporting, routing analytics, pricing model analysis, and performance dashboards automate; local hiring decisions, commercial account relationship management, and regulatory compliance oversight persist as human work. 30% reflects the analytics and reporting slice.

Termite WDO inspection-based selling and door-to-door residential canvassing remain human. Lead handling, automated follow-up, proposal generation, and quote automation are AI-applicable. 35% for the automatable pipeline/quote/follow-up portion.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Housekeeping and Janitorial Workers (37-1011)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Route supervisors and field training roles — physical oversight and technician coaching are not AI-replaceable. AI route optimization and service quality dashboards may modestly widen the span of control, automating reporting aspects. 10% for the documentation and reporting component.

Inbound call handling for scheduling, renewal reminders, and standard service inquiries is highly automatable by AI phone/chat agents. PCT and FieldRoutes surveys confirm AI phone agents are being actively adopted for this function. Residual 20% for complex complaints, cancellation handling, and situations requiring empathy and judgment.
Scheduling, record-keeping, correspondence, and document management — near-fully automatable. 80% with 20% residual for non-routine correspondence and tasks requiring local judgment.
Physical application of pesticides to vegetation — licensed applicator work, not AI-replaceable. 5% token for record-keeping and application documentation tasks.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Admin Support (43-1011)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Office workflow coordination and queue management partially automate; staff supervision and exception handling persist. 35% for the automatable administrative coordination aspects.

Billing, AR/AP, payroll processing, and reconciliation — highly automatable by AI accounting tools. Termite retreat/repair guarantee liability reserves require judgment but are a small fraction. 85% automatable.
The 6.51% unlisted tail is a miscellaneous mix of support roles not individually classified in the OEWS data at 5-digit level. 50% automatable is a neutral blended assumption for an unspecified mix.

pi_dist.rationale: Pest control operators have strong client-relationship ownership at the firm level: (1) Recurring residential accounts attach to the brand/phone number and transfer well in acquisitions — this is the documented driver of pest control roll-up economics; (2) Pricing is set locally by the operator with no upstream payer, auditor, or fee schedule — AI efficiency gains accrue to the operator as margin improvement, not passed through as client price reductions; (3) Commercial contracts for food processing, healthcare, and hospitality require named compliance relationships but these transfer with the business in structured acquisitions. The 0.70 reflects genuinely firm-controlled customer relationships with moderate risk from Google Local Services Ads and national brand advertising competing on customer acquisition at the residential low end.
sources: CT Acquisitions pest control M&A and valuation guides 2025-2026 — recurring residential accounts transfer well in acquisitions
FieldRoutes 2025 State of Pest Industry Report — pricing model and recurring plan mix
The Pest Control Broker valuation guide — commercial account ownership and transfer characteristics

pi_moat.rationale: Meaningful but not exceptional barriers: (1) State applicator licensing restricts new entrant technician supply and creates regulatory compliance overhead; (2) Route density — the geographic clustering of recurring accounts creates a natural local monopoly dynamic in dense routes; (3) Switching costs on residential recurring: once a customer is on a quarterly plan with a service history, they rarely switch absent service failure; (4) Commercial duty-of-care: healthcare and food-service facilities require documented compliance history. Offsets: (a) Google Local Services Ads and large national brands (Orkin/Rollins, Terminix) are pricing aggressively on customer acquisition, compressing local operator margins on new accounts; (b) DIY-plus-AI diagnosis is a nascent but real low-end threat at the residential market; (c) Smart trap/remote monitoring could reduce visit frequency on commercial accounts — reducing revenue per account. Net: 0.65 reflects a genuinely moaty local-services business with identifiable disruptors at the margin.
sources: CT Acquisitions pest control valuation — route density as margin driver
FieldRoutes AI adoption in pest control — smart traps and remote monitoring
PCT Top 100 coverage — national brand pricing competition

t50_years.derivation: Current adoption: ~20% of operators materially using AI in automatable back-office/scheduling/phone functions (FieldRoutes/Thrive April 2025 survey: 27% using AI in any form; conservative adjustment to 20% for 'material use in automatable functions' specifically, excluding marketing-only use). Historical analog: pest control routing/scheduling software adoption — PestPac/FieldRoutes grew from niche to majority adoption in approximately 8-10 years (2010-2018). AI tools are simpler to deploy as SaaS with no hardware requirement, and the industry is demonstrating faster uptake than prior software (18% to 27% in one year per FieldRoutes data). Derivation: from 20% now (2026) to 50% target with adoption accelerating faster than prior software analog — using 5 years (2026 to 2031). The faster pace relative to the 8-year routing software analog reflects the low marginal cost of AI phone agents and scheduling tools, the documented commercial pressure from large acquirers running AI-optimized operations (signaling to targets), and the 27-35% adoption growth rate observed 2024-2026. t50 = 5.0 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: FieldRoutes / Thrive Analytics 2025 State of the Pest Industry Report (survey of 1,025 pest control company leaders, April 2025)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 27% of pest control companies are using AI in 2025, up from 18% in 2024 (Thrive Analytics survey of 1,025 pest control leaders). Conservative downward adjustment to 20% for material use specifically in back-office/scheduling/phone automation functions.

historical_analogs.value: Pest control routing and scheduling software (PestPac, FieldRoutes/PestRoutes) adoption: approximately 8-10 years from early-adopter phase (~2010) to majority operator use (~2018-2020); PestPac currently serves more than 60% of PCT Top 100.
historical_analogs.source: PestPac by WorkWave platform data; FieldRoutes/ServiceTitan acquisition history; PCT Top 100 coverage
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: PestPac serves more than 60% of the top 100 pest control businesses (current); AI adoption grew from 18% to 27% in a single year (2024-2025 FieldRoutes survey)

owners_60plus_pct.source: PCT magazine survey (reader survey, ~70 respondents); Exit Planning Institute cross-industry data; NPMA 2023 Business Profile Study (596 responses, full demographic detail not publicly accessible)
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: In a recent PCT survey, one in two pest control business owners said they've been approached to sell their business; Exit Planning Institute reports over half of Baby Boomer business owners plan to exit within a few years. Pest control industry commentary consistently highlights aging family-owned operators (founded 1970s-1990s) as primary acquisition targets.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: PCT survey: 1 in 2 pest control owners have been approached to sell; majority approached by PE firms. NPMA commentary and Potomac/CT Acquisitions broker analysis consistently identifies aging Baby Boomer ownership as a primary succession driver. The high and accelerating M&A deal volume (97 transactions in 2024, up 27.6% YoY) is partly attributable to succession-motivated owner exits. Succession shortage documented by pattern of evidence across PCT, NPMA, and sell-side advisors.

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions 2026 Pest Control PE Roll-Up Tracker; Capstone Partners Pest Control Sector Update (cited in CT Acquisitions)
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 12 primary PE-backed platforms actively acquiring: Anticimex (EQT), Aptive (Goldman Sachs AM), Hawx (Aurora Capital), ProGuard (Trivest), Greenix (Gridiron Capital), Certus (Imperial Capital+LMI), Rockit Pest (Halle Capital), PestCo Holdings (Thompson Street), AXN Growth Partners (Shore Capital), Barefoot (Incline Equity), plus additional platforms. Certus: 7 disclosed acquisitions in 2024 (most active); Rockit and PestCo: 6 each; AXN and Barefoot: 5 each.

buy_mult.source: Breakwater M&A pest control valuation multiples 2026; CT Acquisitions pest control valuation guide 2026
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Residential pest control $500K-$2M EBITDA: 5.7x-6.9x EBITDA; mid-market $1-3M EBITDA: 5x-7x; 6x-7.5x for documented 70-85% recurring businesses. n_band target (100-499 employee class, avg ~$3.4M EBITDA) is in the 5.5-6.5x range; 6.0x used as median.

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions pest control valuation guide; Breakwater M&A 2026; thedealsheet.co pest control deep dive
exit_mult.figure_quoted: Larger pest operators $3M-$10M EBITDA close at 9-12x; platform-quality operators with 80%+ recurring revenue reach 7x-10x in PE roll-up transactions; Rollins strategic premium 12-18x on transformational deals excluded. 10.5x used as conservative platform exit for $10M+ EBITDA tier.

pricing_structure.value: Recurring per-visit or annual subscription plans for residential (~$300-$600/year per account); annual service contracts for commercial (priced per site per month); one-time termite treatment plus annual bond/renewal fee. Revenue mix: ~65-70% residential recurring, ~20-25% commercial contract, ~5-10% termite/one-time. Pricing set locally by operator with no external payer or auditor. AI efficiency savings accrue to operator margin, not passed to customers via fee reduction.
pricing_structure.source: FieldRoutes pest control profitability guide; CT Acquisitions recurring revenue analysis; PCT State of Industry coverage
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Recurring revenue share is the primary valuation driver; operators with 80%+ recurring contracts command 7.5-10x vs 4-5x for low-recurring operators. Residential plans typically $300-$600/year; commercial contracts billed monthly or quarterly per site.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp=11.04pp substantially exceeds the 3pp floor. A=2.0 (t50=5 years) is below 5, so no cross-check tension. The combination makes sense: high labor_share (0.475) produces meaningful uplift even though only ~23% of labor is automatable, and slow-to-mid adoption pace (A=2.0) is appropriate given the industry's demonstrated but still early AI adoption trajectory.

terminal_value.justification: The pest control product is DURABLE under mature AI. The value proposition — a licensed technician physically treating a structure to eliminate or prevent pest infestation — is not substitutable by AI. AI cannot spray a termiticide or place a bait station; mature AI does not perform the service visit. The automatable surface is bounded to the ~30-35% of wage bill in office, scheduling, sales support, and management reporting around the route. The two edge-case threats identified in the prompt are (1) smart-trap/remote-monitoring reducing visit frequency on commercial accounts and (2) DIY-plus-AI at the residential low end — both are nascent and quantitatively small relative to current revenue, with no documented revenue-per-account erosion yet in industry surveys. The DURABLE classification is supported by the physical, licensed, regulatory-compliance nature of the core service. This is explicitly distinguished from AI cutting operator overhead (real, bounded, captured in V) vs. AI replacing the paid visit (not occurring and not anticipated at scale in the foreseeable horizon).

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V was set from the OEWS role wage_shares and declared within-role automatable fractions based on task analysis of what AI can currently do (scheduling, billing, phone handling, route optimization reporting, bookkeeping). The 0.05 fraction for pest control workers reflects route optimization and documentation assistance only, not visit elimination. A was set from the FieldRoutes/Thrive Analytics 2025 survey on current AI adoption rates and from the pest control routing software historical analog. No deal activity, consolidation trends, or M&A volume data were referenced when setting V or A.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pest control is priced as a recurring subscription or service contract, set locally by the operator with no external payer, auditor, or fee schedule above the firm. This is not a cost-plus or hourly model — when AI cuts back-office and routing costs, the savings accrue to the operator as EBITDA margin improvement, not as automatic price reductions to clients. Competitive pressure from national brands (Orkin, Terminix, Anticimex franchises) and Google Local Services Ads operates on customer acquisition pricing for new accounts, not on the cost pass-through mechanism. This operator-favorable structure is reflected in the high pi_dist=0.70; pi_moat=0.65 reflects the genuine barriers alongside the real competitive threats at the new-customer acquisition stage.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=3: A=3.333, S rises from 3.71 to approximately 4.16. At t50=8: A=1.25, S falls to approximately 3.27. Range of approximately ±0.45 in S across the plausible t50 range.

At owners_60plus_pct=0.35: OW_pre=0.714, +0.1 succession=0.814, B changes moderately. At 0.52: OW_pre already hits 1.2, capped at 0.9+0.1=1.0. With succession bonus already at cap (1.0) for most of the range, OW impact on B is limited above 0.42. Primary uncertainty is in the ESTIMATE quality of the input, not score sensitivity.

At buy_mult=5.0 with exit_mult=10.5: M=clamp(4*5.5/5.0,0,10)=4.4, S rises to approximately 3.94. At buy_mult=7.0: M=clamp(4*3.5/7.0,0,10)=2.0, S falls to approximately 3.38. Range of approximately ±0.3 in S.

evidence.reviewer_note: n_band sensitivity acknowledged per prompt: the 15.65% generic Damodaran margin puts only the 100-499 class (70 firms, avg $3.4M EBITDA) in the $1-10M band. However, well-run pest operators commonly run 20-25%+ EBITDA margins (per CT Acquisitions and FieldRoutes data). At 20% margin, the 20-99 class (766 firms, avg $4.7M revenue) enters the band at approximately $940K EBITDA — borderline in. n_band=70 is therefore treated as a conservative floor; actual addressable roll-up target count is plausibly 2-5x higher. This undercounts B slightly. Termite/WDO retreat-and-repair guarantee liabilities are long-tail obligations requiring QoE reserve — not captured in the simple EBITDA margin used for n_band calculation; noted for acquirer due diligence. Vegetation/lawn pest boundary with 561730 exists but the dominant segment of 561710 is structural pest control and termite work, which drives the economics scored here.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical outdoor field labor — mowing, planting, pruning, installation. Mature AI does not replace physical crew work. Robotic mowers are a robotics-capex story, not an AI-labor substitution channel, and commercial adoption is early.

On-site crew leadership; AI scheduling and job documentation may marginally widen span of control but core on-site supervisory judgment, safety oversight, and real-time crew management are not AI-replaceable. Only a small documentation/reporting sliver is automatable.
Physical skilled outdoor work — climbing, pruning, felling. Not an AI-substitution surface.

Job costing, fleet scheduling, payroll reporting, and routine operational reporting are automatable. Client relationship management, labor management, vendor negotiations, and strategic planning persist. 20% reflects a material but not dominant automatable task fraction.

Invoicing, data entry, dispatch coordination, scheduling, seasonal payroll processing for hourly workforce — predominantly AI-automatable. Small residual in-person reception and physical document handling.

AI aerial-imagery property measurement and automated quoting compress estimating labor substantially. However, relationship selling on commercial accounts, responding to RFPs, and managing client renewals persist. 35% reflects the estimating/takeoff portion being largely automatable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Pesticide Handlers, Sprayers, and Applicators, Vegetation (37-3012)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical application work, licensed in many states. Not an AI-substitution surface.
Physical hardscape/site-prep labor. Not an AI-substitution surface.
Scheduling, communications drafting, document preparation — predominantly automatable. In-person reception component is smaller fraction.

Payroll processing, accounts payable/receivable, job-cost reconciliation — highly automatable with current AI and accounting software. Only final review/sign-off and client-facing accounting judgment persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Remainder/tail (mechanics, drivers, irrigation techs, designers — unlisted occupations)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Equipment maintenance and driving are physical and not AI-replaceable. Landscape design/CAD work has AI exposure (generative design tools) but is a small share of this tail. Irrigation techs are physical. 10% reflects a small design-exposure sliver.

pi_dist.rationale: Commercial maintenance (~60% of band-size firm revenue per Aspire 2025 industry data) is procured by property managers and facility managers, often on annual/multi-year tender bids with competitive re-pricing; the client relationship is held by the property manager or national account procurement. Residential accounts (~30%) have more direct owner relationship. Design-build (~10%) is project-by-project with direct client engagement. Blended pi_dist reflects the substantial gatekeeper force on the dominant commercial segment: property managers capture AI-enabled cost savings at next bid cycle; the residential/direct segment partially offsets this with more direct relationship. BrightView's chronically thin margins (FY2024: 11.7% adjusted EBITDA) confirm limited commercial pricing power despite scale.
sources: CT Acquisitions Landscaping M&A Multiples 2026 (ctacquisitions.com)
Yellowstone Landscape blog on commercial maintenance contracts (yellowstonelandscape.com)
BrightView Holdings FY2024 8-K press release (SEC EDGAR)

pi_moat.rationale: Near-zero entry barriers (truck, trailer, crew) and no licensure requirement for basic landscape maintenance. Switching costs are moderate for commercial accounts (transition timing, quality uncertainty) but low for residential. Route density is a real but replicable operational advantage — any capitalized entrant can build it. No proprietary data or platform moat. H-2B labor visa constraints create systemic supply pressure but affect all operators equally, not a moat. Cheap AI back-office tools becoming universal may actually lower entry barriers further by removing administrative friction. The primary defense is local brand reputation and contract stickiness, rated modestly at 0.40.
sources: CT Acquisitions Landscaping M&A Multiples 2026 (ctacquisitions.com)
Main Street Wealth Landscaping M&A 2026 (mainstreetwealth.ai)

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption (AI in automatable back-office functions — scheduling, estimating, job costing) estimated at 17% per Aspire 2025 Landscape Industry Report. Historical analog: business management/accounting software in landscaping reached ~77% adoption but started becoming prevalent ~2008-2012, implying ~12-15 year diffusion to supermajority. GPS fleet tracking reached only ~42% by 2023 after ~13 years, suggesting slow hardware adoption but faster SaaS. AI SaaS tools have lower friction than GPS hardware. Starting from 2026 with 17% material adoption, reaching 50% by ~2034 implies t50 = 8 years. This reflects faster diffusion than GPS hardware but slower than enterprise software sectors (which saw 5-7 year t50) given the industry's fragmented, small-operator base with high cost and learning-curve sensitivity (Aspire survey: top barriers are cost 50%, lack of time 49%, learning curve 43%).

current_adoption_pct.source: Aspire 2025 Landscape Industry Report / Lawn & Landscape 2026 Tech Report
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 83% of landscaping professionals have not yet adopted AI tools (Aspire 2025); 17% currently using AI. Lawn & Landscape 2026 Tech Report: 32% 'always' use AI tools like ChatGPT, 11% 'usually', 30% occasional — but this counts light/occasional use. Material adoption in automatable functions estimated at 17%.

historical_analogs.value: 10-15 years for business management software and GPS fleet tracking to reach broad adoption in landscaping
historical_analogs.source: Lawn & Landscape GPS tracking article; BLS/industry software adoption trajectory
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: GPS tracking in equipment has 42% penetration in the landscaping industry as of 2023 after ~13 years of availability; accounting software now at 77% after approximately 15 years of broad SaaS availability (2008-2023).

owners_60plus_pct.source: Green Industry Pros survey; Census Bureau employer-business owner age data; Main Street Wealth landscaping M&A analysis
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: Green Industry Pros survey: roughly one-third of established, full-time landscape company owners are over age 55. Census: 52.3% of U.S. employer-businesses owned by people 55+. Main Street Wealth: 'bulk of $5-25M EBITDA landscaping operators are founder-led, and most have no obvious family or management successor.' Estimated 60+ share at ~30% based on 55+ share of ~33% with downward age adjustment.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Main Street Wealth (2026): 'bulk of $5-25M EBITDA landscaping operators are founder-led, and most have no obvious family or management successor.' Green industry M&A advisory sources confirm documented succession gap as key deal driver.

active_consolidators.source: CT Acquisitions Landscaping M&A Multiples 2026; Main Street Wealth Landscaping M&A 2026; web research on PE-backed platforms
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Active PE-backed platforms with documented add-on acquisition activity in last 24 months: BrightView (MSD Partners), Yellowstone Landscape, Sperber (New Water Capital), LandCare (Aurora Resurgence), SavATree (Apax, 'in active acquisition mode'), Mariani Premier Group (CI Capital), Exscape (BHMS, 3 add-ons Dec 2025), Juniper Landscaping, Elevation Landscape Group (Trinity Hunt, launched May 2026), Aspen Grove (Trivest), Heartland Companies, Ruppert Landscape, U.S. Lawns, Schill Grounds Management. Count: 14.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions Landscaping M&A Multiples 2026; BizBuySell Q1 2026 data
buy_mult.figure_quoted: For $2M to $10M revenue landscaping operators (lower-middle-market, corresponding to $1M-$3M EBITDA at 15.65% margin): '5.0x to 7.0x adjusted EBITDA for financial buyers.' Median of this range = 6.0x; using 5.5x to account for the lower end of normalized-EBITDA band-size firms (100-499 employee class, avg implied EBITDA $3.58M). EBITDA normalization applied: buy_mult reflects normalized EBITDA after market-rate owner/operator compensation.

exit_mult.source: CT Acquisitions Landscaping M&A Multiples 2026; PitchBook 2025 landscaping transaction data
exit_mult.figure_quoted: $10M to $50M regional platforms: '7.5x to 10.0x adjusted EBITDA per PitchBook 2025 data.' Median of this range = 8.75x; using 8.5x as conservative median for $10M+ EBITDA platforms. BrightView Holdings trading at approximately 8.1x EV/adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2026 (public comp floor).

pricing_structure.value: Commercial maintenance contracts (~60% of revenue for band-size firms): annual/multi-year fixed-scope agreements with CPI escalators, re-bid competitively on expiration. Residential recurring (~25%): seasonal fixed-price packages or per-visit billing. Design-build/enhancement (~15%): fixed-bid project work. Commercial contracts provide predictable revenue but limited pricing power — property managers hold competitive leverage at bid renewal.
pricing_structure.source: CT Acquisitions Landscaping M&A Multiples 2026; Yellowstone Landscape commercial maintenance blog; Aspire pricing strategies blog
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Commercial maintenance: 'multi-year commercial maintenance contracts with CPI escalators' typical. Yellowstone: contracts include 'automatic renewal provisions' with '3-5% yearly increases.' Commercial segment is largest share for mid-market operators. BrightView: 'maintenance revenue' dominant at ~$2.1B of ~$2.7B FY2025 guidance.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = V_raw × 100 = 3.26pp. A = 1.25 (slow adoption, t50 = 8 years). Uplift is just above the 3pp threshold with A < 5, so no tension flag applies — the cross-check threshold only flags when uplift < 3 AND A >= 5. Both A and uplift are low and consistent: slow adoption of a thin automatable surface is coherent.

terminal_value.justification: Grass grows and beds require maintenance regardless of AI maturity. The ~72% of wage bill in field labor (crews, supervisors, tree trimmers, pesticide applicators, construction laborers) performs irreplaceable physical on-site work. The demand for landscape services is not threatened by AI — it is visit-bound by physical necessity. The automatable surface is the thin back-office/sales wrapper (~8% of total labor costs). Even with robotic mowing (a capex substitution distinct from AI-labor channel), field labor demand survives because installation, seasonal plantings, tree care, and complex maintenance require physical crews. BrightView's $2.7B+ revenue guidance for FY2025 and continued commercial demand confirm sector durability. DURABLE: demand survives mature AI; AI cuts back-office cost only, does not substitute the core product.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V (1.30) was derived entirely from OEWS wage-share breakdown applied to within-role automatable fraction estimates — no reference to deal activity or consolidation trends. A (1.25) was derived from current_adoption_pct (17% from Aspire 2025 industry survey) and historical technology diffusion analogs (GPS tracking, management software) in the landscaping industry — no reference to PE consolidation activity or M&A trends. Confirmed: V and A are justified independently.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pricing structure: commercial maintenance (~60% of revenue) billed as annual/multi-year fixed-scope contracts with CPI escalators, competitively re-bid at expiration by property managers. AI back-office efficiency reduces operator cost but the next bid cycle hands those savings to property managers who can simply invite competing bids at lower prices — this is the primary deflation mechanism. Reflected in pi_dist = 0.35 (property managers hold the client relationship on the dominant revenue segment) and pi_moat = 0.40 (near-zero entry barriers mean AI back-office tools, once ubiquitous, lower entry barriers and intensify competitive bidding rather than protecting margins). BrightView's FY2024 adjusted EBITDA margin of 11.7% — despite scale and technology investment — confirms structural limits to commercial pricing power.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A = 10/t50; at t50=5 → A=2.0; at t50=12 → A=0.833. Range: A from 0.833 to 2.0. S varies from ~1.85 (t50=12) to ~2.17 (t50=5). Moderate impact.
C = 10 × pi_dist × pi_moat; at pi_dist=0.25 → C=1.0; at pi_dist=0.50 → C=2.0. S range from ~1.83 (pi_dist=0.25) to ~2.07 (pi_dist=0.50). Moderate impact.

OW changes from 0.571 (at 0.30 base) to 0.671 (post-succession +0.1 bonus) to range 0.621-0.771 across 0.25-0.40 with succession bonus. B shifts from ~5.57 to ~6.16. S varies ~1.95-2.00. Small impact.

evidence.reviewer_note: V trap avoided: 60%+ of wage bill is physical field labor (37-3011, 37-1012, 37-3013, 37-3012, 47-2061) assigned 0% automatable — this is the defining structural feature. The 15.65% Damodaran margin used for n_band is generous for commercial-maintenance-heavy operators (BrightView runs 11.7% adjusted EBITDA); the true n_band is likely lower, which would reduce B. The buy_mult of 5.5x applies to band-size firms ($3-4M implied EBITDA) on a normalized EBITDA basis; smaller mow-and-blow operators transact at 2-3x SDE, which is irrelevant to the band but shows wide multiple variation within the code. The ai_replaceable_share of ~8% of total labor is deliberately conservative per the prompt's explicit instruction that robotic mowing is a capex story not folded into the AI-labor channel. S = 1.96 reflects a genuine low-score code: high B (active consolidation, succession pressure), but very low V (physical labor dominates), very low A (fragmented, slow-adopting industry), and moderate-low C/M.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
